narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict whether someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated an 85.9% chance that you would not, which is why your application was denied—schools use this to prioritize those predicted to succeed right away. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 22, an undergraduate GPA of 2.50, and planning to attend full-time, which is the most common choice among students. What tipped the scales most strongly toward this high failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking, as being at the bottom there signaled to the model a tougher path to bar success compared to typical students who rank around the middle of their class. Your LSAT score had the next biggest push in the same direction, since 22 is well below the average of about 37 that the model sees in students who pass. Even your first-year decile at the bottom added to this concern, reinforcing a pattern of lower academic standing relative to peers. On a more positive note, your middle family income actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, as it aligns better than lower brackets for many who succeed. Your undergraduate GPA had a small influence pulling toward failure, while your full-time status offered a minor boost since most successful students share that path. Overall, your academic rankings and LSAT stood out as the top factors dragging the prediction down when compared to averages—most admitted students have stronger showings there. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you've overcome as a Black woman in education; it's just the model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience. With targeted improvements in these areas, you could reapply stronger and show the model—and schools—your true capabilities. Keep pushing; your story matters.
